name: Eridu
description: Eridu, also known as Tell Abu Shahrain, stands as one of the oldest cities in Mesopotamia and holds immense significance in Sumerian mythology. It was considered the first city created by the gods, a primordial settlement established before the Great Flood. Eridu was believed to be the home of Enki (also known as Ea), the god of wisdom, intelligence, crafts, and magic, and the protector of humanity. The city was renowned for its temple, the E-abzu, dedicated to Enki and believed to be located near the Abzu, the subterranean freshwater ocean from which all life originated. Eridu represented the dawn of civilization, the establishment of divine order on Earth, and the source of all knowledge and culture. Its priests were considered wise and powerful, acting as intermediaries between the gods and humans. The city's decline in political prominence did not diminish its mythological importance, as it remained a symbol of the origins of Sumerian civilization and the benevolent influence of Enki.
subtitle: The First City, Home of Enki

keyMyths.title: The Creation of Eridu
keyMyths.description: Eridu was created by the gods as the first city, a place of order and civilization amidst chaos. Enki, the god of wisdom, established his temple there, marking it as a center of divine knowledge.

keyMyths.title: Enki and the World Order
keyMyths.description: Enki, residing in Eridu, established the me, the fundamental laws and cultural practices of civilization. He distributed these me to other cities, spreading knowledge and order throughout Sumer.

sources.author: Samuel Noah Kramer
sources.description: Provides insight into Sumerian culture and the importance of Eridu.
sources.text: Provides insight into Sumerian culture and the importance of Eridu.

sources.author: Stephanie Dalley
sources.description: A collection of Sumerian myths, including those related to Enki and Eridu.
